The letter to the Hebrews opens with an urgent claim: God has spoken. Not tentatively or partially, but definitively and finally. For centuries, God addressed His people through the prophets—in visions and dreams, through burning bushes and still small voices, in warnings and promises delivered by human messengers. But now something has changed. In these last days, God has spoken by His Son. This is not another prophet in a long line of prophets. This is the Son, and the distinction matters with absolute weight. To hear the Son is to hear God Himself speaking. The writer does not invite us to debate whether Jesus is important or relevant to faith. He announces that Jesus is the final word from God, the culmination of all previous revelation, the one through whom God has chosen to communicate what we most need to know.

But who is this Son? The author immediately moves beyond the claim of finality to reveal the Son's true identity. He is the heir of all things—not just a recipient of blessing, but the one appointed to inherit everything. More strikingly, He is the one through whom God made the universe. Before time existed, before anything was created, the Son was active in creation itself. He is not a being who came into existence; He is the eternal power by which all existence came to be. He is the radiance of God's glory, the exact representation of God's nature—not a reflection or an echo, but the precise and perfect expression of who God is. This Son upholded all things by His powerful word. The universe does not hold itself together. It is sustained moment by moment by the Son's word. And then, in time and in history, this eternal Son became human, provided purification for sins through His death, and sat down at the right hand of God in triumph. The writer confronts us with the staggering reality: the one who created and sustains all things also became human and died to cleanse us from sin.

The author then drives this point home through an extended series of Old Testament quotations, each one establishing the Son's superiority. Angels—those mighty beings of light and power—are called winds and flames of fire, servants of God's will. But the Son is called God. "Your throne, O God, endures forever and ever," the Psalmist declares, and the author applies these words to Jesus. God calls the Son "Father," a relationship never granted to any angel. All God's angels are commanded to worship Him. The Son is eternal while creation will fade; He remains unchanged while the heavens and earth wear out like old clothing. He sits at God's right hand while His enemies await their defeat. This is not poetry meant to inspire us spiritually. This is doctrine meant to reorient our entire understanding of who Jesus is and what He has accomplished. To follow Christ is to follow the one who holds all authority, who sustains all things, and who has secured our salvation through His death and resurrection.

If this is true—and the author stakes everything on the claim that it is—then our response cannot be casual. The Son is not one option among many spiritual paths. He is not a helpful figure to add to our existing beliefs. He is the exact representation of God's nature, the radiance of God's glory, the one through whom and for whom all things exist. Every moment you live is sustained by His word. Every sin you have committed has been atoned for by His sacrifice. Every promise of God finds its fulfillment in Him. The angels themselves have been commanded to worship Him. And the author writes to tell you: this Son has spoken. He is speaking. The question is not whether God has revealed Himself. The question is whether you will listen.
